data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-13T01:51:00.933Z</current_datetime>

Help me write MSTest tests for this formatting utility. I want to use the right MSTest string assertion methods like StartsWith and EndsWith to validate the output formats.

```csharp
namespace Contoso.Text;

public sealed class StringFormatter
{
    public string FormatPhoneNumber(string digits)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(digits);
        if (digits.Length != 10)
            throw new ArgumentException("Must be exactly 10 digits.", nameof(digits));
        return $"({digits[..3]}) {digits[3..6]}-{digits[6..]}";
    }

    public string BuildGreeting(string name, string title = "Mr.")
    {
        ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrWhiteSpace(name);
        return $"Dear {title} {name},";
    }

    public string GenerateSlug(string title)
    {
        ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrWhiteSpace(title);
        return Regex.Replace(title.Trim().ToLowerInvariant(), @"[^a-z0-9]+", "-").Trim('-');
    }

    public string FormatCurrency(decimal amount, string symbol = "$")
    {
        return $"{symbol}{amount:F2}";
    }
}
```

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to write MSTest tests for a `StringFormatter` class. They specifically want to use MSTest string assertion methods like `StartsWith` and `EndsWith`. Let me check what MSTest version supports these - in MSTest v3+, `Assert.That.String()` provides `StartsWith`, `EndsWith`, `Contains`, etc.

Let me first check the current working directory and see if there's any existing project structure.

data.content: MSTest 4.0.2 — has `StringAssert` with `StartsWith`, `EndsWith`, `Contains`, and `Matches`. Let me create the source and tests.
data.reasoningText:
MSTest 4.0.2 - great. In MSTest v3+, the string assertions are available via `Assert.That.String()` which provides methods like `StartsWith`, `EndsWith`, `Contains`, `Matches`, `DoesNotContain`, etc.

Wait, let me think about the exact API. In MSTest v3, it's `StringAssert` class that provides `StartsWith`, `EndsWith`, `Contains`, `Matches`. Actually, `StringAssert` has been available since MSTest v1. Let me check whether MSTest v4 has newer APIs.

Actually, `StringAssert` is the classic MSTest class with methods:


- `StartsWith(string value, string substring)`
- `EndsWith(string value, string substring)`
- `Contains(string value, string substring)`
- `Matches(string value, Regex pattern)`
- `DoesNotMatch(string value, Regex pattern)`

There might be newer `Assert.That` extensions in v3/v4, but `StringAssert` is the standard approach here. Now I'll write out the source and test files.

data.content: All 32 tests pass. Here's the test file:

**32 tests, all passing.** The tests use all five `StringAssert` methods:

| Method | Used in |
|---|---|
| `StringAssert.StartsWith` | Phone area code, greeting "Dear", currency symbol |
| `StringAssert.EndsWith` | Phone last 4 digits, greeting comma, currency decimals |
| `StringAssert.Contains` | Phone dash, greeting name/title, slug content, negative sign |
| `StringAssert.Matches` | Phone format regex, slug character set, currency pattern |
| `StringAssert.DoesNotMatch` | Slug has no uppercase |

Each method also has error-path tests using `Assert.ThrowsExactly` for null/invalid inputs.
